license_note: See Wikimedia Commons file page for license.
description: Riverside bastion and ceremonial gateway to Lisbon's harbour, decorated with Manueline rope motifs and royal shields.
facts: UNESCO-listed with the nearby Jerónimos Monastery as a monuments ensemble.
Spiral stairs to the terrace can queue heavily before noon.
history: Built from lioz limestone as part of King Manuel I's coastal defence and prestige programme.
significance: Icon of Portugal's Age of Discovery imagery.

license_note: See Wikimedia Commons file page for license.
description: Hieronymite monastery funded by spice-trade wealth, housing Vasco da Gama's tomb and a filigree stone cloister.
facts: Pastel de nata queues at Antiga Confeitaria de Belém sit a short walk east.
Church nave is free; cloister and museum sectors are ticketed.
history: Linked to the return of da Gama's first India voyage; the complex became a state pantheon for navigators.
significance: High point of Portuguese Late Gothic carving.

license_note: See Wikimedia Commons file page for license.
description: Yellow arcaded square opening to the Tagus, framed by government wings and the triumphal Rua Augusta Arch.
facts: Equestrian statue of Joseph I anchors the riverside centre.
Trams to Alfama skirt the square's north side.
history: Replaced the royal palace destroyed in the 1755 earthquake as the city's ceremonial river gate.
significance: Default festival ground and tourist orientation hub.

license_note: See Wikimedia Commons file page for license.
description: Vertical iron link between lower Baixa grid and Carmo terrace viewpoints, still using original carriages on busy days.
facts: Vista tickets at the top may require an extra queue beyond lift fare.
Night lighting emphasises riveted neo-Gothic tracery.
history: Steam originally powered the lift; electrification integrated it into Carris urban transport branding.
significance: Industrial heritage selfie spot between grid and hill neighbourhoods.

license_note: See Wikimedia Commons file page for license.
description: Hilltop fortifications with ramparts overlooking terracotta roofs, cruise ships on the Tagus, and peacocks in gardens.
facts: Archaeological trenches expose Iron Age and Roman layers inside the walls.
Sunset crowds peak on the eastern battlements.
history: Moorish citadel became royal palace; today's ruins are largely medieval and restored 20th-century fabric.
significance: The compass rose for understanding Lisbon's seven hills layout.

description: Wood-and-brass cars squeal through Alfama curves past miradouros and cathedral approaches.
facts: Pickpockets target crowded summer rides.
Early morning offers calmer photography than midday crush.
history: Electrified lines replaced funiculars on some hills while keeping narrow gauge.
significance: Moving postcard through the steepest historic quarters.
license_note: See Wikimedia Commons file page for license.

description: Blue-and-white tile panoramas including Lisbon pre-earthquake cityscape panels.
facts: Café sits in the former convent cloister.
Bus links faster than walking from Santa Apolónia.
history: Madre de Deus church absorbed into a national azulejo collection.
significance: Best single-site survey of Portuguese tile painting.
license_note: See Wikimedia Commons file page for license.

description: Street art, design shops, and rooftop bars inside a former textile mill yard under the bridge.
facts: Sunday markets add food trucks to the bookshop courtyard.
Evening Uber queues spike after concerts at the bridge arena.
history: Lisbon's post-industrial waterfront strip attracted creatives before major hotel chains.
significance: Alternative retail spine west of Belém.
license_note: See Wikimedia Commons file page for license.

description: Whispering-gallery dome over tombs of presidents, fado singers, and navigators.
facts: Feira da Ladra flea market spills around the square on Tuesdays.
Wind at the terrace can be strong above the Alfama roofscape.
history: Unfinished church became a secular pantheon for national heroes.
significance: 360° roof views reward the climb from the tile quarter.
license_note: See Wikimedia Commons file page for license.

description: Roofless nave houses an archaeological museum under surviving pointed arches.
facts: Elevador de Santa Justa lands almost at the gate.
Earthquake interpretation panels line the lateral chapels.
history: 1755 shockwave stripped vaults; romantic ruin aesthetic drew 19th-century visitors.
significance: Physical reminder of Lisbon's seismic vulnerability.
license_note: See Wikimedia Commons file page for license.

description: Riverfront contemporary art and technology shows with roof walks toward Ponte 25 de Abril.
facts: Sunset reflects along the black mirror cladding.
Combined tickets sometimes pair with Central Tejo next door.
history: EPAL power station neighbour negotiated shared public esplanades.
significance: Belém's modern wing beyond the monastery axis.
license_note: See Wikimedia Commons file page for license.

architecture_style: Portuguese baroque façades and wave pavement
description: Central plaza with baroque fountains, theatre, and station portal feeding Sintra trains.
facts: Café Nicola writers' tradition still draws journalists.
Elevador da Glória climbs toward Bairro Alto at the west edge.
history: Inquisition autos-da-fé gave way to civic festivals and café culture.
significance: Default meeting point between Baixa and nightlife hills.
license_note: See Wikimedia Commons file page for license.
